Caption: CHVRCHES DJ Set at SXSW — Maggie Mae's, Austin, 2016-03-10
Facts: - CHVRCHES performed a DJ set at SXSW on 2016-03-15, scheduled at 7:15pm at Maggie Mae's in Austin, Texas. [1]

Caption: "A Work of Fiction" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- "A Work of Fiction" supplied the final title of the Vicious Creature album; Mayberry had originally wanted to name the entire album "Fiction" before the phrase "a work of fiction" — taken from this track's lyric — was adopted as the album's title instead. [1]
- The song was co-written with Ethan Gruska. Mayberry told Gruska she wanted "a Tori Amossy piano part"; he said he didn't listen to Tori Amos much, then produced an immediately striking piano part that Mayberry felt delivered exactly what she had in mind. [1]
- Ethan Gruska had previously worked with Phoebe Bridgers; Mayberry described him as "really amazing and such a lovely talented person." [1]
- Mayberry described the song as having "a kind of hazy nostalgia," writing about memories she associates with both good and bad forms of nostalgia. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Austin City Limits Festival, October 2014
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Austin City Limits Festival for the first time on 2014-10-10 (Weekend 2; ACL 2014 ran over two weekends: Oct 3–5 and Oct 10–12). [1,2]
- CHVRCHES were listed on the ACL 2014 Weekend 2 live-stream schedule on 2014-10-10 in the 5:15 p.m. CST slot on Channel 1, as part of a Red Bull TV partnership with ACL organisers. [2]
- Lauren Mayberry identified ACL 2014 as approximately the band's eighth or ninth visit to the United States. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry described ACL 2014 as the last stop of The Bones of What You Believe touring cycle. [1]
- Martin Doherty stated that the ACL 2014 visit was "hands down the best one yet" in reference to the band's US visits overall. [1]
- Martin Doherty stated that by the end of the TBOWYTB touring cycle, CHVRCHES had developed a habit of buying doughnuts or popsicles (ice lollies) for fans who queue from midday for evening shows. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry noted that the last US festival CHVRCHES had played before ACL 2014 was SXSW "at the start of last year" (i.e., early 2013). [1]
- Martin Doherty stated at ACL 2014 that the second album would prioritise songwriting first and that the overall sound had not yet been determined, adding "I'm quite excited by that because it's more fun that way." [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Austin City Limits Festival, October 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Austin City Limits Festival on 2018-10-13, their second appearance at the festival's Honda stage. [1]
- The partial setlist included: "Get Out" (opener), "Bury It", "Gun", "Graffiti", "Miracle", "Deliverance", "Forever", "Recover", "Leave a Trace", "Clearest Blue", "The Mother We Share"; "The Mother We Share" was described as the penultimate song. (List is partial.) [1]
- Lauren Mayberry called Texas senator Ted Cruz a "cock popsicle" from the stage. [1]
- Mayberry dedicated "Leave a Trace" to women survivors of sexual assault and abuse, saying from the stage: "This is for all the ladies who have survived something. Because it is not our job to get walked over by men." [1]

Caption: "Addicted To Love" (CHVRCHES Single, January 2026)
Facts: - "Addicted To Love" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2026-01-16 on Hollywood Records, per the australian-charts.com singles discography; this is the first corpus source to confirm Hollywood Records as the label for 2026 CHVRCHES material. [1]

Caption: Aereogramme Dissolve After 2007 US Tour
Era: My Heart Has a Wish That You Would Not Go
Facts:
- After a draining US tour in 2007, Aereogramme dissolved. Iain Cook described it as "the most depressing month of my life." [1]
- The 2007 tour was Aereogramme's return to the US after a few years away; the band had hoped to "plug back into" the buzz from 2003, when they released the Sleep And Release album, but Cook said: "And by the time we actually came back, it was all fucked, and nobody gave a shit." [1]
- Martin Doherty had recently joined Aereogramme as a touring keyboardist when the band dissolved, experiencing the psychological breakdown of the collapse without the context of having watched the band's trajectory deteriorate over time. Cook described Doherty as having been "thrown right into the middle of it." [1]
- A pivotal moment on the final Aereogramme US tour was a canceled show in Dallas: Cook and the band arrived at the venue, and Campbell McNeil (the band's bassist and later CHVRCHES' manager) went inside to check in. The cleaning lady told McNeil the show had been canceled because no one had bought any tickets — that was how the band found out. Cook recalled: "The cleaning lady was like, 'The gig's been canceled. No one was interested in buying any tickets. That's how we found out that we had no gig that night! We were like, 'Shit. This really has been bad'." [2]
- During the final Aereogramme tour, Doherty and Cook were rooming together on the road. After a particularly terrible night, they resolved to pursue a new direction. Cook stated: "We sat down after a particular terrible night and were like, 'This is crap. Can't we do something that people might actually pay to listen to and want to hear?' I think that was the point for me when things began to change. Enough of this. We got this out of our system. It was a blast, but another six weeks in a smoke-filled van full of beardy guys is not how I want to spend the rest of my life. I've done it for 10 years, and I've had enough of it'." [2]

Caption: Afterglow (Every Open Eye — closing track)
Facts:
- The lead vocal on "Afterglow" was recorded in a single take during the Every Open Eye sessions. Mayberry: "It's the one song on the album that's a once-through vocal." She elaborated in a separate interview: "With 'Afterglow', I think if you had a really over-produced, overdone vocal on that song, it would be too much. It needs to have that rawness about it." [1,2]
- The band fell out over "Afterglow" for approximately six weeks during the Every Open Eye recording sessions. Doherty: "We all have a great relationship but we fell out over that song for about six weeks. We really butted heads." Mayberry: "I was like, 'get away from me with that trance beat'." [2]
- On the last day of the Every Open Eye recording sessions, the song was pulled from the shelf, slowed down, its drum track deleted, and the lead vocal re-recorded in a single pass. [2]
- The single-take vocal captured prominent ambient room sounds. Mayberry: "It's weird how much noise you make... All these swallowing, breathing sounds. There's a bit where you can hear the radiator clinking! But I like that — all those things give it personality. There's a lot of vocals you hear on the radio that don't have any character." [2]
- Witnessing Mayberry's vocal performance made Doherty emotional; the song went from nearly being discarded to closing the album. Doherty: "Witnessing Lauren's performance on Afterglow actually made me quite emotional. And we were like 'right, that's closing the record'. It went from the bin to one of the most important pieces of music on the album." [2]
- Mayberry: "The record wouldn't be the same without it." [2]
- The SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio characterises "Afterglow" (alongside "Down Side Of Me" and "Clearest Blue") as being about "moving on to better things, holding on to the good and letting go of the bad." This represents the band's/label's own promotional framing of the song's lyrical theme. [3]

Caption: "Anywhere But Dancing" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry described "Anywhere But Dancing" as deliberately evoking the style of Frightened Rabbit: "I want to do that kind of like a Frightened Rabbitty type." She added: "Obviously would never could never hold a candle to that, lyrically or otherwise." [1]
- The song is about Scotland and about where Mayberry is from; the lyrical focus on place was a deliberate reason to give it a Frightened Rabbit-influenced sonic character. [1]
- Mayberry described the song as "not really about me necessarily," distinguishing it from the more directly autobiographical tracks on the album. [1]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry Debut Solo Single "Are You Awake?", September 2023
Facts:
- In September 2023, Lauren Mayberry released her debut solo single, "Are You Awake?" — described by AllMusic as an "inward-looking ballad" and by NPR's All Things Considered as "a slow piano ballad that sounds nothing like the electro-dance-pop of Chvrches." The single was released just a few days before Mayberry's first solo concert on 2023-09-04. The Skinny (April 2024) confirms the song was produced in collaboration with Canadian artist Tobias Jesso Jr., described as the first-ever GRAMMY winner for Songwriter of the Year and a fellow LA resident. [1,2,3]
- At the time of releasing "Are You Awake?" in September 2023, Mayberry assured fans that CHVRCHES was still a going concern. [1]
- Mayberry had first announced details of her solo material in July 2023, describing it as "fun, freaky, sad, weird, joyful," before revealing "Are You Awake?" as her debut single. [4]
- Mayberry described the origin of "Are You Awake?" as a moment of self-doubt written on a rainy day in Los Angeles: "I don't know what you're doing here – this is a terrible idea. You've missed everything in everyone's lives because you've moved away to make this thing and maybe that's dumb. Everyone else has made the right decisions and you've chosen the wrong things to focus on. You're an idiot." She added: "Then I got a song out of it!" [4]
- Mayberry described the decision to release "Are You Awake?" first rather than a more uptempo track: "something that was so not in the Chvrches universe would be the best thing to start with to cleanse the pallet and scorch the earth"; she noted her initial instinct had been "'Let's get the fucking bangers going!'" Explaining the same decision backstage at her first solo concert (2023-09-04), she added: "something that's so different is a good palate cleanser. It kind of, like, scorches the earth... But then there's definitely more bangers." [4,2]
- Mayberry described the production of "Are You Awake?" as deliberately sparse: "It's not hidden behind any production; there's just a handful of elements. To me, that feels terrifying." She also clarified the debut solo album would "not...be an album of piano ballads." [4]
- "Are You Awake?" was written on a rainy afternoon in Los Angeles in approximately 2022-12, co-written by Mayberry and Tobias Jesso Jr. Both were away from home in Los Angeles; Mayberry noted "he's not from there and I'm not from there." Neither had been home for two Christmases in a row due to COVID-19. [5]
- "Are You Awake?" was performed live at the Òran Mór Q&A in Glasgow on 2024-12-08. [5]

Caption: CHVRCHES Perform with BBC Scottish Symphony Orchestra, BBC Scotland Channel Launch, 2019
Facts: - In 2019, CHVRCHES performed with the BBC Scottish Symphony Orchestra to commemorate the launch of the BBC Scotland television channel. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES on the BBC Sound of 2013 Shortlist
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were included on the BBC Sound of 2013 shortlist, ultimately finishing fifth; the listing was part of the band's pre-album buzz-building period. [1,2,3,4]
- The BBC Sound of 2013 longlist was compiled from tips from more than 210 tastemakers, described as music critics, editors, broadcasters, and bloggers. [3]
- The BBC Sound of 2013 top five was revealed in reverse order, one artist per day, with CHVRCHES announced at number five on 2012-12-31 and the winner to be announced the following Friday. [3]

Caption: "Bury It" (Song and Alternate Version Featuring Hayley Williams)
Facts:
- Every Open Eye's singles included a version of "Bury It" featuring Paramore's Hayley Williams. [1]
- The SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio characterises "Bury It" (alongside "Never Ending Circles" and "Playing Dead") as being about "being unapologetic and not being told what to do or who to be." This represents the band's/label's own promotional framing of the song's lyrical theme. [2]

Caption: "By the Throat" (Song)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- "By the Throat" was track 11 (penultimate track) on The Bones of What You Believe; Martin Doherty reflected that by the album's release day he had already noticed the clapping elements in the track had been "quite trendy at the time" and had "not dated as well." [1]
- The song was originally going to be called "Bad Blood"; CHVRCHES changed the title after receiving a heads-up from Bastille — who share the same record label — that Bastille were releasing a song with that title. Shortly afterward, Taylor Swift also released a song titled "Bad Blood," prompting Mayberry to reflect: "Phew! Good thing we changed that!" [1]
- Lauren Mayberry stated on the record that "By the Throat" is among a handful of CHVRCHES songs written about the experience of signing the band: "Obviously, we were so lucky for what was happening... But all that glitters isn't always gold, necessarily. What that experience actually entails — you think that everybody in your life is gonna be really happy for you... it isn't like that, in my experience." She noted that at the time she couldn't say this directly to her bandmates "because it'll scare them," so she wrote it as a song instead. [1]
- Iain Cook noted a lyrical thread connecting "By the Throat" to "How Not To Drown." [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Cover Tegan and Sara's "Call It Off" for The Con 10th Anniversary Edition
Facts: - CHVRCHES covered Tegan and Sara's "Call It Off" for a compilation benefiting the Tegan and Sara Foundation — the tenth anniversary edition of The Con. Lauren Mayberry confirmed in a February 2018 interview: "We did a cover of Call It Off for their compilation that benefits the Tegan and Sara Foundation." [1,2]

Caption: Campbell McNeil — Biographical Facts (CHVRCHES Manager)
Facts:
- Campbell McNeil, former bassist of Aereogramme, became CHVRCHES' manager. [1,2]
- After the end of Aereogramme, McNeil worked as tour manager for The Temper Trap and Gotye before taking on the CHVRCHES management role. [1]

Caption: "Change Shapes" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Single
Facts:
- "Change Shapes" is a Lauren Mayberry solo track produced in collaboration with Matthew Koma. [1]
- The Skinny (April 2024) described "Change Shapes" as a "full blown pop belter." [1]
- "Change Shapes" had been released via EMI by 2024-04-29, the date of The Skinny's Lauren Mayberry solo feature; the article's closing note states "Change Shapes is out now via EMI." [1]
- Mayberry co-wrote "Change Shapes" with a collaborator named Evan Blair and a person named Cleo Taylor (Mayberry's pronunciation of the second name was transcribed as "Lasso Cleo Taylor" in the source; the exact first name is uncertain). [2]
- Mayberry described "Change Shapes" as "definitely the most pop pop one" on the album. [2]
- "Change Shapes" includes the lyric "I'm a doll inside a box, with a ball and a chain"; BBC describes the song as a condemnation of music industry sexism. [3]
- While recording the chorus of "Change Shapes," Mayberry was already thinking about choreography: "I quite like the idea of like some kind of choreography girl band thing here." [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES Appear in Archie Comics
Facts:
- CHVRCHES' likenesses appeared in an Archie comic; the band confirmed this in a February 2018 interview. Martin Doherty attributed the opportunity to the band's well-known interests in video games and comics resonating with creators: "that kind of filters, I guess, through to people that make comics and make video games. So we've ended up putting songs on video games and here we are finally, our faces in a comic. And that's just one of those amazing opportunities that comes across and you get asked if you want to do it and you say, hell yeah." [1]
- As of February 2018, Tegan and Sara were announced to appear in a new Archie comic following the CHVRCHES appearance; Lauren Mayberry stated: "I think Tegan and Sara are going to be there in a new one." Martin Doherty confirmed: "Yeah, they are." [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES — Studio Sessions for Vic Galloway's BBC Programme, 2012 and 2014
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts: - CHVRCHES recorded studio sessions for BBC Scotland broadcaster Vic Galloway's BBC programme in both 2012 and 2014. Galloway referenced photographs from both sessions while hosting the Òran Mór Q&A on 2024-12-08. No further details — song titles, specific programme name, or exact recording dates — are confirmed from this source. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at the Thekla, Bristol (Bluetooth Mouse Incident)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- During an early CHVRCHES show at the Thekla in Bristol (approximate date: the early touring period of 2012–2013), the band's Bluetooth mouse — stored in a flight case at the side of the stage — was being moved around by sub-bass vibrations, causing the cursor to jump uncontrollably on screen during the intro to "Lies." Cook was unable to advance to the verse section of the song: "I couldn't get control of the mouse cursor to move onto the next section of the song." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry, watching the incident from the stage: "I thought you'd lost your damn mind." [1]
- Mayberry stated in 2023 that she has never returned to the Thekla Bristol venue since this show. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Support Death Cab for Cutie on US Tour, June 2016
Facts: - CHVRCHES performed on a short US support tour with Death Cab for Cutie in June 2016, preceding their Mexico concert dates later that year (El Plaza Condesa CDMX on 2016-09-28; Live Out Festival Monterrey on 2016-10-01). [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Open for Depeche Mode — Four European Shows, July 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- In July 2013, CHVRCHES opened four European shows for Depeche Mode — on the Delta Machine tour — whom the band have cited as heroes and aesthetic forebears. [1,2]
- Iain Cook said he was overcome with "sheer terror" in the lead-up to the Depeche Mode support shows. [1]
- Cook said of hearing Depeche Mode open with songs from Violator at the first support show: "When they played some of the songs off that, the first show, I was nearly in tears." He described Violator as "one of my favorite records of all time." [1]
- Cook said: "When they put out Violator, I was 11 or something" — the article notes he was "off by a few years." Violator was released in March 1990; Cook was born approximately 1975 (age 38 in September 2013), making him approximately 15 when the album was released. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at El Plaza Condesa, Mexico City — 28 September 2016
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at El Plaza Condesa in Mexico City, Mexico on 2016-09-28; the show sold out, with all tickets selling out approximately two months after going on sale. [1]
- Floor ticket resale prices reached approximately $2,000 MXN ahead of the show; the Ticketmaster website did not release additional tickets, as occasionally happens with sold-out events. [1]
- Doors opened slightly before the scheduled 7:00pm opening time; CHVRCHES took the stage at 9:30pm. [1]
- The pre-show ambient playlist featured music by Radiohead, Deftones, Deafheaven, Holly Herndon, DJ Shadow, and Caribou. [1]
- The stage featured a CHVRCHES logo banner as backdrop; Moog synthesizers and electronic instruments were covered with black cloths before the show. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry appeared on stage in a deliberately simple outfit: ripped jeans and black boots. [1]
- The complete 18-song setlist in order: (1) Never Ending Circles / (2) We Sink / (3) Keep You on My Side / (4) Make Them Gold / (5) Empty Threat / (6) Tether / (7) Playing Dead / (8) Science/Visions / (9) Lies / (10) Gun / (11) High Enough to Carry You Over / (12) Under the Tide / (13) Recover / (14) Bury It / (15) Leave a Trace / (16) Clearest Blue; Encore: (17) Afterglow / (18) The Mother We Share. [1]
- "Never Ending Circles" opened the show; the reviewer identified it as the standard concert opener for the Every Open Eye touring cycle. [1]
- During "Empty Threat" and "Playing Dead," Lauren Mayberry played percussion on stage, as she does in regular live performance; during "Playing Dead" she ended the song lying on the floor. [1]
- For "Tether," Iain Cook switched from bass and synthesizers to electric guitar. [1]
- "Science/Visions" was performed and described by the reviewer as a surprise return, noting it had not been played for some time prior to this show. [1]
- Between "Lies" and "Gun," Lauren Mayberry delivered a brief spoken address to the crowd. [1]
- A Mexico flag bearing the CHVRCHES name written in black was thrown from the crowd to Martin Doherty at the transition before "High Enough to Carry You Over"; he placed it on his midi controller, then swapped positions with Lauren Mayberry and took lead vocals for "High Enough to Carry You Over" and "Under the Tide." [1]
- "Recover" was performed with its full intro. [1]
- "Afterglow" opened the encore and was performed nearly a cappella, with Mayberry delivering the song with minimal backing. [1]
- During the encore's closing song "The Mother We Share," Lauren Mayberry wore the Mexico flag that had been thrown to Martin Doherty earlier in the set, wearing it throughout the song. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry verbally promised from the stage at the end of the concert to return to Mexico to present the third album. [1]
- The concert lasted approximately ninety minutes. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES — Fifth Studio Album (In Development, 2026)
Facts:
- Mayberry stated on 2023-09-04 (backstage at her first solo concert, for NPR's All Things Considered) that CHVRCHES had re-signed with their record label for more albums: "We've re-signed the band for more records. So everyone is confident and comfortable that that's going to happen." She also confirmed: "Our plan is that we're going to do both" — meaning the solo career and the band. [1]
- On the temporary nature of the CHVRCHES hiatus, Mayberry stated in September 2023: "good for everyone to fly the coop for a little bit and then come back together." [1]
- In a 2026-02-17 Billboard interview by Sophie Williams, CHVRCHES described their forthcoming fifth studio album as having "a ferocity to it" — an attributed on-the-record characterisation of the album's sonic or creative direction; the full article body is not captured in the corpus, so the complete quote and its speaker are not confirmed. [2]
- Mayberry first referred to a fifth CHVRCHES album on the record in October 2023, stating: "Then, as and when we come back for the fifth album, it has to be moving the conversation forward – it can't just be a different version of the same thing." [3]
- At the Òran Mór Q&A on 2024-12-08, Mayberry again confirmed CHVRCHES' ongoing status, saying the band had re-signed and were figuring out next steps: "Yes, so we've just taken a wee pause, but we've — the band's resigned and we're figuring out what to do." She added: "Yes. I feel like I should have like a tabard with a disclaimer on it, but I'm just going around at the moment like, sorry, it's fine, it'll happen again." This corroborates her September 2023 statement that the band had re-signed for more records. [4,1]
- Mayberry articulated the rationale for the CHVRCHES hiatus in October 2023: "After 10 years and four albums, this is a good time to take a minute. Everyone go on your own adventure so when we come back we're not just having the same musical conversations over and over." She also stated: "Four albums in a row is pretty solid, so it's important to get new stuff into our brains." [3]
- Mayberry offered a direct public reassurance about CHVRCHES' future in October 2023: "Just give us a couple more years guys, it's going to be fine." [3]
- In the summer of 2025, CHVRCHES first publicly told fans they had started work on a new album, without sharing any details about a potential release date. [5,6]
- In an NME interview published in early March 2026 ("earlier this month" relative to 2026-03-27), Martin Doherty and Jonny Scott discussed the forthcoming fifth CHVRCHES album. Doherty stated: "It's going to surprise people, and I hope it's going to delight them and rip some faces off," and confirmed the album was "90 per cent complete" at that time. He added: "It's quite hard, but not to the point where you won't recognise the band. It's still the same people and DNA." [5]
- Jonny Scott is referenced alongside Martin Doherty as a participant in the early March 2026 NME interview about the fifth album, described by NME as part of "the band." This is the first source in the corpus to indicate Scott may hold a band-level (rather than touring) role in the fifth album cycle. [5]
- CHVRCHES released two commercial singles in early 2026 on Hollywood Records, per the australian-charts.com singles discography: "Addicted To Love" (Australian release 2026-01-16) and "Such Great Heights" (Australian release 2026-02-17) — the first corpus source to confirm Hollywood Records as the label for the 2026 CHVRCHES recording cycle. [7]

Caption: CHVRCHES First Concert at the Art School, Glasgow, July 2012
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- Martin Doherty stated in a 2013 interview that the first official CHVRCHES concert was held at the Art School in Glasgow in July 2012. [1]
- Martin Doherty described the first CHVRCHES concert as "an exciting and terrifying time," noting that despite having played in many different bands previously, he had never felt such intense nerves as at that show. [1]
- A German concert review published 2012-07-26 describes the Art School show as CHVRCHES' first headliner show under their own name in early July 2012; it was preceded by a single pseudonym appearance under "Shark Week" at a Vic Galloway event in Edinburgh, which was not counted as an official CHVRCHES performance. [2]
- Backstage before the show, the trio did something they said they would never repeat: each took a shot of tequila to calm their nerves before going on stage. They knew label representatives would be in the audience. Martin confirmed in a 2016 interview that this was "the only time I've ever drank tequila before a performance. To this day" — and Iain added that even Lauren, who had not been expected to partake, took a shot. [3,4]
- At the first Art School show, an A&R representative from a UK label took Mayberry aside and said: "This is going to be amazing. I'm going to make you the new Pixie Lott." Mayberry described her reaction: "I'm sure she's a lovely lady, and what she's doing is great for her. But at that time I was like, 'But I'm in a band, and you've just come in and decided that you're going to separate me from the band and put the girl up front'. That stuff was very uncomfortable for me." [3]

Caption: CHVRCHES First Los Angeles Show, March 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES' first-ever show in Los Angeles (March 2013) was sold out far in advance and organised by Neon Gold Records; the band described performing at a Neon Gold-organised event as an "honor," as Neon Gold were "the first people to play their song." [1]
- A Paste Magazine interview by Philip Cosores was conducted with all three CHVRCHES members — Lauren Mayberry, Iain Cook, and Martin Doherty — outdoors in Echo Park, Los Angeles, on the evening before the first LA show (approximately 2013-03-24, consistent with the article's first publication date of 2013-03-25). [1]
- The morning after the first LA show, CHVRCHES flew from Los Angeles to Austin, Texas to perform at South by Southwest (SXSW). [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Founded — Lauren Mayberry Invited to Join
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- In September 2011, Iain Cook and Martin Doherty invited vocalist Lauren Mayberry to sing on some demos — specifically synth-oriented material, despite each member's background in guitar-oriented bands; at the time, Mayberry was singing and drumming in Blue Sky Archives, another Glasgow indie rock band that Cook had produced an EP (the Triple A-Side EP) for. Lauren described the sequence from her perspective in 2016: "I met Iain when he was recording an EP of my old band and then he had mentioned that him and Martin were working on some stuff and then I went in to have a listen and that's pretty much where it started." [1,2,3,4,5]
- Prior to recruiting Mayberry for the project, Cook primarily knew her as the usher at a local cinema he frequented; his professional knowledge of her came through producing the Blue Sky Archives EP. Cook described his decision as: "He recognized Mayberry as a distinctive vocalist and an engaging personality" and intended her role initially as backup vocals to contrast with Doherty's lead voice — she could also play keyboards if the band eventually performed live. [6]
- Mayberry arrived 45 minutes late to her first recording session with Cook and Doherty — something she later recalled with embarrassment. [6]
- Mayberry became lead vocalist through an accidental discovery: during a session the lead vocal (Doherty's) was deleted for an unspecified reason, leaving only her harmony/chorus vocal audible. Cook recalled: "We were going through the tracks and deleted the lead vocal for some reason, and her voice was still in there in the chorus. And we went 'Holy shit!' Martin and I just looked at each other, like, 'Okay, this is better than we thought it was'." Cook and Doherty then asked Mayberry directly whether she would want to be the lead singer; she said "Yeah, cool." [6]
- The sessions with Mayberry went so well that the trio immediately finalised their roster and christened themselves CHVRCHES. Cook said: "When you have three personalities, you get shit done. If there's four or five, it's harder." The name was chosen to be spelled with a "v" instead of a "u" both for its "visual possibilities" and to create a Google-resistant name; the band's logo, stage lighting, and posters are adorned with luminous 45-degree angles. [1,2]
- CHVRCHES began writing songs inspired by Prince, Depeche Mode, and Kate Bush. [1]
- Martin Doherty's original inspiration for wanting a female vocalist was Jenny Lewis's role on the Postal Service record. Doherty stated in March 2013: "We thought we wanted a female vocal and initially we wanted to try it for backing vocals. For me it was coming from a kind of Postal Service-y place, like the stuff that Jenny Lewis did on that record." In 2023 he was more emphatic: "Without that record [Give Up], there is no Chvrches." [7,3]
- When Mayberry first came in, Doherty was initially inspired to have her provide backing accompaniment to his own lead vocals, mimicking the dynamic between Jenny Lewis and Ben Gibbard on The Postal Service's Give Up. He brought her in to record backing vocals, then muted his own vocals during playback so she could hear her parts on their own. Cook recalled the moment the sound came together as: "It was almost like the sound jumped out, fully formed. It was like, 'This is the next 12 years of your life.'" Doherty said he discovered she had "the perfect electronic voice" — "The frequency was so pure that you could drive the bass and go as far as you like with the production." [3]
- Martin Doherty described the three-way collaboration with Mayberry as an equal and unprecedented partnership: "when we started working with Lauren, we entered it as a three-way partnership in ways that I've never seen with anyone, and I didn't think would be possible." [7]
- Paste Magazine (first published March 2013) states that the band "had come together properly near the end of 2011, writing as a three-person team"; this describes the consolidation of the band as a committed unit and is consistent with the initial invitation to Mayberry in September 2011 as the start of a process that concluded by late Q4 2011. [7]
- The early CHVRCHES project took shape at Alucard Studios, a basement flat in the south side of Glasgow that Cook was renting for his film and TV scoring work; it was conceived initially as a private studio experiment with no aspirations of becoming a public act. Cook: "At this time it was just a studio project with no aspirations of it being a band or anything more than just us sort of fannying about in a basement." [8]
- Cook's first-person account of recruiting Mayberry, given in a 2015-10 Sound on Sound interview: "I had cheekily played Martin a couple of rough mixes. We were both like, 'Holy shit, she's got a brilliant voice.' I asked her if she wanted to come down and listen to some stuff." This is the most detailed first-person telling by a band member of the circumstances of Mayberry's recruitment. [8]
- Doherty recalled the founding sessions in a 2015-10 interview with the approximate date "I guess it was 2012 when we eventually got in the studio and just thought, 'Let’s throw some ideas around.'" ⚠️ Conflicts with: multiple earlier sources (AllMusic, Paste Magazine March 2013, Consequence of Sound September 2013) consistently date the founding to late 2011 — Doherty’s "I guess" signals approximate recollection four years after the fact. [8]
- Mayberry described her initial concern about the collaboration: "My fear initially was that I was going to go in there and it would be two guys who had written the stuff and they just wanted me to sing it. But it wasn't like that at all, and that was definitely a relief." She also noted: "We were really fortunate that each of our strengths matched up to make this easy." [9]
- Doherty described the early songwriting phase: "There was something really exciting about that time. There was no attention on the band, no-one knew who we were. We were doing it all behind the scenes." [9]
- The BBC EOE promotional feature (ca. 2015) states the band "only got together in November 2011," specifying November as the formation month. ⚠️ Conflicts with: the `date: 2011-09` frontmatter, which is derived from Under the Radar and Stereogum sources placing Mayberry's first invitation to sing in September 2011; the BBC account may refer to the formal constitution of the band as a committed writing unit, consistent with Paste Magazine ("came together properly near the end of 2011") rather than the initial invitation. [10]
- Doherty, in an interview attributed to Pitchfork, described the band's mixed backgrounds as a strength that sceptics had initially seen as a weakness: "The stuff that people perceived as potential weaknesses in the beginning — a bunch of random folk, different ages, different backgrounds, that we weren't all skinny 19-year-olds — ended up becoming our biggest strength. People could see that it was real." [10]
- From the outset, the band maintained a policy that they appear together as a band for media interviews or not at all, declining to send Lauren Mayberry alone to press engagements. [10]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Glastonbury Festival, June 2023
Facts: - During CHVRCHES' performance at Glastonbury 2023, Lauren Mayberry wore an ivory gown with feather trim from the RIXO bridal range and ended the set doused in fake blood, the once satin-shiny dress smeared with stains of vivid red. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Hordern Pavilion, Sydney, 22 July 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Hordern Pavilion in Sydney, Australia on 22 July 2018 as part of Australian sideshow dates following their appearance at Splendour in the Grass 2018. [1]
- Support acts for the show were Wafia (opening) and Mansionair (second support). [1]
- The CHVRCHES set lasted approximately one and a half hours. [1]
- The partial setlist (combined from two sources) included at least: "Get Out," "Bury It," "Graffiti," "High Enough to Carry Me Over," "Recover," "Leave A Trace," "Clearest Blue," "God's Plan," "My Enemy," and "The Mother We Share." [1,2]
- Lauren Mayberry did not attend soundcheck for this show, resulting in pitch difficulties early in the set; she remarked on stage: "I didn't go to soundcheck, I had some personal shit." [1]
- As the band was preparing to play "The Mother We Share," Mayberry spotted a fan wearing a Celine Dion shirt in the front row and performed an impromptu rendition of Celine Dion's "Don't Think Twice"; Mayberry joked: "If you guys ever need a wedding band…" [1,2]
  - Note: [1] describes the trigger as "band T-shirts in the crowd" without specifying Celine Dion; [2] provides the more specific detail.
- Lauren Mayberry appeared on stage "christened in glitter" for this show. [2]
- Martin Doherty shared vocal duties with Lauren Mayberry on "My Enemy" during the show, covering the Matt Berninger studio part (the article refers to the song as "The Enemy"). [2]
- $1 from each ticket for this show went to Girls Rock! Sydney, an organisation that runs a holiday camp empowering girls, trans, and non-binary youth through music. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Lincoln Hall, Chicago, September 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES played Lincoln Hall in Chicago on a Monday night in approximately mid-September 2013; the show was an addition to the tour tacked on due to high demand and sold out. [1]
- The Lincoln Hall show was the first of two consecutive weekday nights CHVRCHES played in Chicago during the September 2013 leg of their tour; Lincoln Hall is described as one of the city's "premiere big-room venues." [1]
- At the time of the Lincoln Hall show, CHVRCHES had traveled to Chicago from Minneapolis. [1]
- Author Steven Arroyo interviewed Lauren Mayberry, Martin Doherty, and Iain Cook in the upstairs room at Lincoln Hall in the afternoon before soundcheck; Mayberry arrived with a Walgreens store-brand steam inhaler and both she and Arroyo were recovering from colds. [1]
- A touring ping-pong table was visible in the pit area at Lincoln Hall; the band took it everywhere on tour, and Martin Doherty was the acknowledged best player. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at LKA Longhorn, Stuttgart, 11 November 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at LKA Longhorn in Stuttgart, Germany on Sunday, 11 November 2018, as part of the Love Is Dead European tour. [1]
- The show at LKA Longhorn was not sold out; the reviewer notes that British indie synth, rock, and pop acts have difficulty filling venues in Stuttgart. [1]
- The stage was sparsely arranged: Martin Doherty and Iain Cook were positioned at the back at two synthesizers/keyboards respectively, Jonny Scott at drums behind them, with a bank of spotlights as the backdrop and the front of the stage left empty. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry wore a black tutu-style dress, a pink t-shirt, and dark eye makeup at the Stuttgart show. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry announced from the stage that she had flu, with her nose, forehead, and ears full of "snot," and asked the audience to translate the word into German; rather than tea with ginger and honey, she was drinking whisky as a substitute. [1]
- The complete 17-song setlist at LKA Longhorn on 11 November 2018 was: Get Out / Bury It / Gun / We Sink / Graffiti / God's Plan / Under the Tide / Miracle / Science/Visions / Really Gone / Deliverance / Forever / Recover / Leave a Trace / Clearest Blue (main set closer); encore: The Mother We Share / Never Say Die. [1]
- Support was provided by Let's Eat Grandma. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Margaret Court Arena, Melbourne, 23 July 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Margaret Court Arena in Melbourne, Australia on 2018-07-23, the night after their Hordern Pavilion Sydney show on 2018-07-22, as part of Australian sideshow dates following their appearance at Splendour in the Grass 2018. [1]
- Support was provided by Mansionair; Wafia was removed from the bill on the day of the show because a production vehicle broke down between Sydney and Melbourne, forcing set times to be pushed back. [1,2]
- ⚠️ Conflicts with: [1] quotes Mayberry referring to "one of the buses carrying all of this stuff"; [2] independently describes the vehicle as "the truck with all of Chvrches' production."
- Doors were originally scheduled to open at approximately 7:30pm but opened at 8:50pm due to the breakdown; CHVRCHES took to the stage at 10:00pm. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry addressed the breakdown on stage: "One of the buses carrying all of this stuff broke down somewhere between Sydney and Melbourne, so I'm gonna talk a whole lot less tonight and we're just gonna play as many songs as we can until curfew." [1]
- Mayberry also remarked on stage: "Our gear didn't get here until 8:30 tonight. I'm just going to talk less and we'll cram in as much as we can before the curfew." [2]
- Fans were left waiting outside the venue for approximately one hour longer than expected while a soundcheck was under way inside. [2]
- The set comprised 15 songs including encores. The partial setlist included at least: "Get Out," "Gun," "Bury It," "God's Plan," "Under the Tide," "Miracle," "Clearest Blue," "The Mother We Share," and "Never Say Die"; "Clearest Blue" closed the main set, and the encore comprised "The Mother We Share" (from The Bones of What You Believe) and "Never Say Die" (from Love Is Dead), with "Never Say Die" closing the night. [1,2]
- Lauren Mayberry wore a black tutu and had glitter on her cheeks during the performance. [2]
- During "Never Say Die," Mayberry sang unamplified into the crowd for the closing lines, with the audience chanting along. [2]
- This was the final date of CHVRCHES' Australian tour. [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Metro, Chicago, September 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES played the Metro in Chicago on a Tuesday night in approximately mid-September 2013; the show sold out and the concert was webcast live via multiple video cameras. [1]
- The Metro show was the second of two consecutive evenings CHVRCHES played in Chicago; the Metro is described as one of the city's "premiere big-room venues." [1]
- CHVRCHES' Maschine MK1 died approximately two hours before the Metro show; after putting out a Twitter plea, a local fan lent the band a replacement unit, making the show possible. Lauren Mayberry thanked the fan from the stage during the show. [1]
- As of the Metro show in September 2013, CHVRCHES had approximately 50 minutes of total material; Mayberry told the audience in advance that there would be no encore. [1]
- The Metro set closed with "The Mother We Share." [1]
- During the show, Lauren Mayberry engaged Martin Doherty in a "semi-coherent rant" about Kevin Costner's 1992 film The Bodyguard, specifically about being paranoid that one of the red lights on the webcasting cameras was a laser sight like in the film. [1]
- After the Metro show, the band travelled onward to Columbus. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at the Metro Theatre, Sydney, 4 February 2014
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at the Metro Theatre in Sydney on 2014-02-04. [1]
- Support was provided by Elizabeth Rose, who had recently toured with Chairlift and Snakadaktal. [1]
- The set included at least: "We Sink", "Gun", "Under The Tide", "Strong Hand" (b-side), "Now Is Not The Time" (non-album), and closed with "By The Throat" (partial list). [1]
- During the show some male audience members shouted obscene and sexually charged comments directed at Lauren Mayberry. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Concert, Night Before Anchor Shop TV Interview, North America, March 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- At this show, an unplanned bubble effect came from the roof of the venue; the band had not requested or anticipated it. Lauren: "There was like bubbles that came out of the roof, which is a bit weird... At no point did I think, say, no bubbles, or please, loads of bubbles." Martin: "Luckily, we enjoyed the bubbles." [1]
- Lauren described CHVRCHES' live performance philosophy by explicitly naming and rejecting the alternative: "Technically, you could just go in with a laptop, push play, and then quietly sit in the back of the stage wearing a pair of sunglasses and drinking a beer. But that's not really something that we're interested in." [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at O2 Academy Bournemouth, 12 February 2019
Facts:
- Let's Eat Grandma, described as an experimental pop duo from Norwich, supported CHVRCHES at this show. [1]
- The set opened with "Get Out" followed by "Bury It"; this is a partial setlist and only the first two songs are confirmed. [1]
- Mid-set, the band discussed their experiences touring in the United States and contrasted social behaviours there with Scotland, particularly in relation to drinking. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at The Ogden Theatre, Denver, 7 August 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at the Ogden Theatre in Denver, Colorado on 7 August 2018 as part of the Love Is Dead tour. [1]
- A flash rainstorm caused a power outage at the Ogden Theatre shortly before doors were due to open, delaying the start of the show. [1]
- The partial setlist for the main set included "Graves," "God's Plan," "Under the Tide," and "Clearest Blue" (main set closer); the encore consisted of "Mother We Share" and "Never Say Die." [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Oxford Art Factory, Sydney, August 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at the Oxford Art Factory in Sydney, Australia in August 2013 as part of their first Australian tour; the venue was reportedly packed. [1]
- At the time of this show, CHVRCHES had released an EP and were described as being "just about to release" The Bones of What You Believe, which was released in October 2013. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES San Francisco Show, March 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts: - At a CHVRCHES show in San Francisco in March 2013 — the night before their first-ever Los Angeles show — Lauren Mayberry jokingly played a couple of bars from the Phantom Planet theme song to the TV series The O.C., prompting an unexpected mass singalong from the crowd. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Stereo, Glasgow, 22 July 2012 (supporting School of Seven Bells)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Stereo, Glasgow on 22 July 2012 as support act for School of Seven Bells; a German-language review published four days later describes this as the band's second ever official live show. [1]
- The support slot lasted approximately 30 minutes and comprised 7 songs; "Lies" was placed last as the climactic highlight. [1]
- The set included at least: "Recover" (described as notably calm and quiet compared to the other tracks) and "Lies" (set closer). Seven songs in total were performed; the remaining titles are not named in the source. [1]
- The debut album was already fully recorded and finished by the time of this show, providing sufficient material to fill the support slot. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry's microphone cut out during "Lies"; she took Martin Doherty's microphone (referred to in the German source as "Doks Mikrofon") and completed the performance. [1]
- The stage setup was Lauren Mayberry center stage, flanked by Iain Cook and Martin Doherty at keyboards on left and right. [1]
- The sound at Stereo was described by the reviewer as exceptionally clear for a basement club venue. [1]
- The crowd was notably large relative to expectations for an opening act. [1]
- The reviewer described Lauren Mayberry's stage presence as slightly shy and diffident, contrasted by wave-inspired makeup. [1]
- After the CHVRCHES set, the reviewer spoke with Iain Cook and Simon Liddell (described in the source as guitarist of Olympic Swimmers) in the upstairs bar at Stereo. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Capital One House (Antone's), SXSW 2019, Austin, Texas
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at the Capital One House at Antone's in Austin, Texas on 2019-03-09 (Saturday) as part of SXSW 2019. [1]
- Before the main downstairs show, CHVRCHES performed a short acoustic set in the upstairs banquet room at Antone's for approximately 200 early-arriving attendees. [1]
- The partial setlist for the acoustic set included "Miracle" (from Love Is Dead, 2018) and "The Mother We Share" (from The Bones of What You Believe, 2013), plus an improvised verse-and-chorus cover of Phil Collins' "Against All Odds." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry remarked "It's all we have!" on stage during the acoustic set, apologising for the mostly melancholic material. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Sydney Opera House, Sydney — 7 January 2019
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Sydney Opera House, Sydney, Australia on 2019-01-07 (Monday); the show was the final date of the band's Australian touring run alongside Falls Festival 2018/2019. [1]
- The reviewer reported that CHVRCHES "never thought they'd play Sydney Opera House," indicating the booking was regarded as a milestone by the band. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry performed in a black tutu. [1]
- Martin Doherty took centre-stage mid-set while Mayberry moved to keys. [1]
- Named songs from the setlist include (partial): "Graffiti" (described as "newest single"), "Recover" (from The Bones of What You Believe), "The Mother We Share" (encore), and "Never Say Die" (encore). [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES One-Night Solo Show, Tokyo, February 2016
Facts: - CHVRCHES performed a one-night solo show in Tokyo in February 2016; the show sold out. Venue is not specified in this source. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Webster Hall, New York City, September 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Webster Hall in New York City the week after their two-night Chicago run in September 2013. [1]
- The Webster Hall show was webcast by MTV Hive. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Coachella, April 2016 (Main Stage)
Facts:
- CHVRCHES played the main stage at Coachella in April 2016; by Martin's description this represented a step up from their previous Coachella slot, which had been on a smaller stage. Martin said: "Now to level up to the main stage, it's different again." [1]
- The CHVRCHES set was scheduled at approximately 18:10 (described as "10 past 6" by Martin and as "6 o'clock" by the interviewer). [1]
- Lauren noted that the 2016 slot avoided direct sunlight, contrasting it with a previous CHVRCHES Coachella appearance where the sun was directly on the stage — which she described as "the warmest show I think we've ever played." [1]
- Guns N' Roses headlined Coachella on the same night as CHVRCHES' main stage set; Iain predicted the crowd would include "a lot of Guns N' Roses t-shirts" and Martin confirmed he intended to stay for their set. [1]
- The night before the CHVRCHES Coachella main stage set, Martin Doherty left the festival site before LCD Soundsystem finished their set, in order to be prepared for a 10am engagement and the evening show; he described LCD Soundsystem as "one of my favourite bands" and said leaving early was deliberate self-discipline. [1]
- Martin described his experience at previous Coachella appearances: "I remember last time we played being pretty blown away by that [the view of the ferris wheel, the mountains, and the skyline]." [1]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry Performs "Cry, Cry, Cry" Duet with Coldplay, March 2023
Facts: - Lauren Mayberry performed a duet with Coldplay on a song called "Cry, Cry, Cry" around March 2023; a Billboard article by Gil Kaufman published 21 March 2023, headlined "Coldplay's 'Cry, Cry, Cry' Duet with Chvrches' Lauren Mayberry Sparks Tears of Joy," reported on the event. The venue, format (live or studio), and full context are not captured in the Billboard headline alone. [1]

Caption: "Conman" (Song, CHVRCHES Fifth Album)
Facts:
- "Conman" is a track from CHVRCHES' forthcoming fifth studio album; it received its world premiere live performance at Robert Smith's Teenage Cancer Trust concert at the Royal Albert Hall on March 27, 2026 — the first time the band had "played this in front of human beings," per Lauren Mayberry's stage announcement. [1]
- The opening lyrics of "Conman" as delivered at the live debut: "A man made for the microphone fills my pockets full of stones / All of his wives, all of his nine lives dance on the edge of a knife." [1]
- NME describes "Conman" as opening "with a pulsing, industrial bass" and unfolding "with grating guitar riffs and urgent, stirring vocal cries from Mayberry, introducing a heavier, post-punk sound for the band." [1]

Caption: Iain Cook and Martin Doherty's Pre-CHVRCHES Musical Collaboration
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- Iain Cook and Martin Doherty met at Glasgow's University of Strathclyde in 2003: contrary to many published accounts that described them simply as having "met at university," Doherty was in fact a student in a class Cook was lecturing. Cook's attention was caught by the PJ Harvey shirt Doherty was wearing. Doherty said: "Initially, I would always look to Iain, and my first instinct in the studio would be, 'What do you think?' Like I was asking a tutor." [1]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: AllMusic and Consequence of Sound (September 2013) describe the t-shirt as a "Paul Harvey" shirt, while Stereogum (2023) identifies it as a "PJ Harvey" shirt. PJ Harvey (the musician) is the more contextually plausible referent for two Glasgow-based indie musicians, and the Stereogum account is the more detailed and direct recollection; Paul Harvey (US radio broadcaster) is an unlikely bonding point. The earlier sources may contain a transcription error. [2,3,1]
- The Cook–Doherty musical partnership began when Doherty asked Cook to produce his band Julia Thirteen; though little came of the sessions, the two continued to work together. [2]
- After the Julia Thirteen sessions, Cook and Doherty both became members of the band Aereogramme; Cook served as guitarist and Doherty as live keyboardist and guitarist. [2,4]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: Paste Magazine (March 2013) characterises Doherty's contribution to Aereogramme as "engineer and contributing musician" rather than live keyboardist and guitarist — the two characterisations may describe different aspects of his involvement (live performance vs. studio work). [5]
- Cook and Doherty had both left Aereogramme by 2007. [2]
- After leaving Aereogramme, Cook became a composer for film and television. [2]
- Seeking a change from alternative rock, Cook and Doherty founded their own experimental synth project — the foundation for what became CHVRCHES. [2]
- In 2007, Cook and Doherty made a deliberate decision to start working on something entirely new. Cook stated: "We sat down in 2007 and decided to set some time and do something fresh, that wasn't from the same place from which we have always come — the indie guitar-rock stuff." [5]
- Following the collapse of Aereogramme's final US tour, Cook bought a Moog Voyager synthesizer and began exploring electronic composition; this triggered the direction that became CHVRCHES. [6]
- Doherty described the years of parallel project work before CHVRCHES: "We've been friends for 10 years. We'd always worked on different projects, but never really done anything that we could both put our names to." The studio they eventually used as the foundation for CHVRCHES was Alucard Studios in Glasgow, which Cook was renting for his scoring work. [7]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Festival Corona Capital, Mexico City, 2014
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts: - CHVRCHES performed at the 5th edition of the Festival Corona Capital in Mexico City in 2014; this was CHVRCHES' first performance in Mexico. [1]

Caption: "Crocodile Tears" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Single
Facts:
- "Crocodile Tears" was released as a single from the Vicious Creature album. [1]
- Mayberry described "Crocodile Tears" as "probably the most '80s synthy" track on Vicious Creature, adding that she was deliberately sparing with '80s synth sounds on the album: "I was very careful about where to spend my '80s synth, because I'm really — I don't want to do it too much, don't want to ruin the legacy of the band." [1]
- Mayberry stated she had held the title "Crocodile Tears" for a long time before finding the right way to execute the song; her creative intent was for it to be "theatrical and sassy" rather than mean. [1]
- Mayberry cited Annie Lennox's first solo work as an influence on the sound of "Crocodile Tears": "we were thinking about that in like, that kind of Annie Lennox, her first solo stuff, like that kind of thing." BBC identified the specific reference as the "choppy, sampled strings of Annie Lennox's Walking On Broken Glass." [1,2]
- Mayberry stated she is role-playing in "Crocodile Tears," inspired by "the dark, subversive femininity of Velma Kelly in the musical Chicago, or Cabaret's Sally Bowles." [2]

Caption: "Death Stranding" (CHVRCHES contribution to Death Stranding: Timefall, 2019)
Facts:
- In 2019, CHVRCHES contributed the single "Death Stranding" to the album Death Stranding: Timefall, a collection of songs inspired by Hideo Kojima's action video game Death Stranding. [1]
- "Death Stranding" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2019-10-04 on RCA Records, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. [2]

Caption: "Deliverance" (Song)
Facts:
- "Deliverance" appears on Love Is Dead (2018). In promotional material for the album (translated from German), it was described as representing "the classic CHVRCHES mix of punk and pure pop." [1]
- Iain Cook described the influences behind "Deliverance" during Love Is Dead promo (circa 2018, translated from German): "You don't hear anyone on the radio today singing about the hypocrisy of religion. But that's what makes it so exciting. When writing the piece, we thought of Madonna's 'Like a Prayer,' Nick Cave, and Depeche Mode's 'Songs of Faith and Devotion'." (German original: "Im Radio hörst du heute niemanden mehr über die Heuchlerei von Religion singen. Aber das ist doch das, was daran so spannend ist. Wir haben beim Schreiben des Stücks an 'Like A Prayer' von Madonna, an Nick Cave und Depeche Modes 'Songs Of Faith And Devotion' gedacht.") [1]

Caption: Down Side Of Me (Every Open Eye track)
Facts:
- The middle-eight of "Down Side Of Me" features round-singing with multi-part harmonies that proved particularly demanding to record. Lauren Mayberry and engineer David Simpson managed the sessions by means of a strict vocal schedule spread across six or seven days, working half-day shifts rather than full days to preserve vocal quality. Mayberry: "Just so you're working over the course of six or seven days, but not all day, every day." [1]
- The SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio characterises "Down Side Of Me" (alongside "Clearest Blue" and "Afterglow") as being about "moving on to better things, holding on to the good and letting go of the bad." This represents the band's/label's own promotional framing of the song's lyrical theme. [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES Contribute "Get Away" to the Drive Re-Scored Soundtrack
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts: - CHVRCHES contributed the track "Get Away" to the re-scored soundtrack of Nicolas Winding Refn's 2011 film Drive; according to AllMusic's biography, this contribution occurred later in the year of The Bones of What You Believe's release, placing it in late 2013. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Elsewhere, Brooklyn, May 2018 (Love Is Dead Tour opening run)
Facts:
- CHVRCHES played two consecutive shows at Elsewhere in Brooklyn in May 2018, described as the first two shows of the Love Is Dead tour, in the days immediately before the album's release on 2018-05-25. [1]
- The partial setlist from the second Elsewhere show (as reported by the interviewer and confirmed in conversation) included at least: "Get Out", "Miracle", "Graffiti", and "Never Say Die". [1]
- "Graffiti" received its first ever live performance at the first of these two Elsewhere shows (described as a Saturday). A fan at the second show was observed singing the lyrics back, despite the song having only been played live the previous night. Lauren Mayberry: "we only played it on Saturday for the first time." [1]
- During one of the Elsewhere shows, Martin Doherty nearly struck Lauren Mayberry with the headstock of his bass guitar after misjudging the size of the stage while moving toward the drummer. Doherty: "I would usually, you know, go next to the drummer if I'm playing the bass and kind of swung the headstock of my bass just as Lauren came careering towards me. It was pretty close." [1]

Caption: Every Open Eye — Recording Sessions, Glasgow, January–June 2015
Facts:
- CHVRCHES finished recording Every Open Eye in June 2015; the Pitchfork studio feature (Laura Snapes, 2015-06-22) was written on the day the band completed the album: "Moments before I arrive at their front door on a typically cold early June afternoon in Glasgow, the trio finally finished recording their second album." The final masters were signed off in early July 2015, on the day of the Under the Radar interview. [1,2]
- The band returned to their Glasgow studio in January 2015 — approximately six weeks after wrapping the TBOWYTB tour in December 2014 — and recorded over approximately five months. They declined multiple offers to work with in-demand producers and engineers in London or Los Angeles, choosing instead to return to their Glasgow basement studio and invest their recording budget in renovating it and buying rare and vintage synthesizers. [1,2]
- Every Open Eye was recorded in the same Glasgow basement flat where CHVRCHES have recorded nearly all of their music since forming in late 2011; the journalist describes it as "really just a basement flat — the same space where Chvrches have recorded nearly all of their music since forming in late 2011." This description is consistent with consequences.net's reference to "Cook's basement studio" for TBOWYTB, and may resolve the apparent conflict with AllMusic's label "Alucard Studio/Alucard Studios" — these names may refer to the same space. [1]
- Every Open Eye was as-yet-untitled at the time of the June 2015 interview. [1]
- Every Open Eye was mixed by Mark 'Spike' Stent, whose résumé includes Madonna, Depeche Mode, Beyoncé, and Björk. The band left Stent for 10 days to mix the album independently, then travelled to his studio for revisions, completing a full album's worth of notes in approximately a day and a half. Doherty described Stent's vocal production as "outstanding": "It's not always brickwalled, it's quite dynamic at times." Mayberry valued that Stent "kept the character and the personality of the vocal" and "left enough space around it." [1,3]
- Dave Simpson served as recording engineer on Every Open Eye; Mayberry referred to him by name during the interview: "I asked [engineer] Dave [Simpson] about it." Before the EOE sessions, Simpson occupied Alucard Studios' second room; CHVRCHES offered him a role on the record in exchange for vacating it. Doherty: "We didn't evict him, but we asked him if we could take over the other room, and traded that for a job on the record. He's a great guy, a supreme talent. He's the only person that we trusted to come in and take charge of some of the technical aspects of what we were doing." [1,3]
- Iain Cook used Cubase as the DAW during the EOE recording sessions, confirmed by the journalist noting Cook "fires up Cubase" to play back finished tracks during the studio visit. The switch to Cubase from Steinberg Nuendo — used for TBOWYTB — was made because the band felt Nuendo had too many features unnecessary for their workflow. [1,3]
- The band spent their TBOWYTB advance on synthesizers; one room of the studio was flanked by more than a dozen keyboards. The EOE synth arsenal included a Roland Jupiter 8, an Oberheim OBXa, a Korg Polysix, a DSI Prophet 12, a Korg ARP Odyssey reissue ("peppered throughout"), and a Moog Sonic Six — a blue-faced, suitcased forerunner of the Minimoog that proved extremely difficult to operate: Cook and David Simpson spent approximately two hours on their first day just getting a note out of it. Doherty: "all the synthesizers I've wanted since I knew what synthesizers were." [1,3]
- Every Open Eye has no live drums; Iain Cook stated: "There are no live drums. Keeping it real — or unreal." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry's vocal booth in the second Alucard room was a prefab foam enclosure — described by the band as "a big foam shed that goes over mic stands" — with no door; Mayberry had to step into it from the floor, placing her water and tea on the ground first. The journalist separately characterised it as "a flimsy purple foam thing that looks liable to collapse." [1,3]
- The recording process began by building songs up to as many as 80 tracks before stripping back; this approach was challenged by Martin Doherty's study of Quincy Jones and Michael Jackson's lean arrangements. Doherty cited three specific albums — Off the Wall (1979), Thriller (1982), and Bad (1987) — as a guideline for trimming unnecessary texture: "At the start, we built up these [songs] with 80 tracks, but then you put on fucking 'Smooth Criminal' or 'Billie Jean' and you're just like, 'Whoa, those only have eight tracks per song — how do we get to that? There's way less in those songs than you would ever imagine, because they sound fucking huge.'" Doherty described the underlying goal as "trying to borrow that sense of economy, those lean arrangements, understand why that worked and try and apply it to what we were doing." [1,2,3]
- The last song recorded for EOE, confirmed as "Afterglow," eluded the band for weeks; they repeatedly added synth layers before realising they should strip it back to just Lauren Mayberry's vocals and a "glowing organ." It was rescued on the last day of recording and became the album closer. [1,4]
- The recording schedule was constrained by the band's upstairs neighbours' sleeping schedules, the only external restriction on the self-funded sessions. [1]
- The EOE writing process followed a structured alternating-sprint pattern: for the first two weeks, the band constructed the skeleton of a new song every day; the following two weeks they returned to revise, adding and subtracting. They then repeated the process with a new set of tracks. Doherty described it as a process of addition by subtraction. [2]
- Martin Doherty reflected on the challenge of over-layering that shaped the EOE recording approach: "When I was younger and first producing records, it was the classic thing where logically I felt the way to get the world's biggest keyboard sound was to put one on top of the other, on top of the other, on top of the other. When you get overexcited in the studio, like, 'This melody excites me, and so does that one, and so does that one,' you have to ask yourself 'Does there really need to be four hooks in this song? A rhythmic hook, a keyboard hook, a vocal hook—all of which are pulling in different directions?' The answer, invariably, is 'no'." [2]
- Every Open Eye was sequenced with two sides of vinyl in mind. The first half features five consecutive upbeat tracks, any of which could be singles; the second half shifts to a moodier register, starting with Martin Doherty's lead-vocal track "High Enough to Carry You Over," followed by "Bury It," and closing with "Afterglow." Iain Cook stated the sequencing structure borrowed a page from Kate Bush's The Hounds of Love. [2]
- Lauren Mayberry struggled with self-doubt during the EOE writing sessions, trying different songwriting voices and disliking all her experiments. She eventually convinced herself that if the words were coming from her own brain they must reflect her genuine self, and her output immediately improved: "As soon as I had that conversation with myself I wrote the lyrics for a whole song on a train in about 35 minutes." [2]
- Iain Cook stated the only concrete goal going into EOE was for everything to be "20 percent different, bigger, better." [1]
- Martin Doherty stated the goal was to avoid making a self-consciously "mature" album: "After making one record that people really like, some bands reject the things that everyone liked about them and make some really deep, thoughtful, dark record — but I wanted to avoid making a 'mature' album. That said, it's not like we're making saccharine shite. There's important lyrical content, and we're still pushing the same emotional boundaries, but also trying to make it as accessible as possible." [1]
- During EOE's recording, multiple parties offered to co-write with CHVRCHES but the band declined each time. Doherty: "I'm over this whole fucking co-write thing that's going on right now. Is no one making records for themselves anymore? Is it not about a band anymore? It's only about putting a voice in a room with the hottest writer. As we were making this album, a bunch of people offered to write with us, but we wanted to be an actual band." [1]
- For Every Open Eye, CHVRCHES operated two recording suites simultaneously: Cook and Doherty worked in the main Alucard room on production and keyboard parts while David Simpson tracked vocals with Lauren Mayberry in the second room at the same time. Doherty: "If we only had the one room and the one rig, it would've taken us much longer to put the record together." [3]
- The main studio room at Alucard was fitted with a pre-existing box-in-box floating room; very little acoustic work was done to the second room used for EOE vocal recording. [3]
- The Neumann KH120A monitors in the main Alucard room were used for both TBOWYTB and Every Open Eye. For EOE, the Tannoy sub used during TBOWYTB was replaced with a matched Neumann KH120A sub. Cook: "We had a Tannoy sub on the first record, but we upgraded to the proper matched Neumann sub on this one." [3]
- EOE vocals were recorded through a Blue Bottle microphone fitted with a C6 capsule — chosen over a C7 after blind tests — routed through a BAE Audio 1073 preamp and a Tube-Tech CL1B compressor at approximately 3:1 ratio. Cook: "We were never smashing the vocals too hard on the way in, just kind of 3:1." [3]
- For EOE beat production, the band preferred recording audio snippets of patterns running from hardware drum machines rather than triggering them via MIDI. The hardware used included a Dave Smith Instruments Tempest analogue drum machine and a Roland TR-8; the TR-8 runs internally at 96kHz. [3]
- The Roland Jupiter 8 was the dominant synthesizer on Every Open Eye. Doherty: "It became a thing where I just naturally gravitated towards that synth every day... it still kind of is all over the album, but it's feature sounds, really deliberate stand-out moments." Doherty explicitly preferred the hardware Jupiter 8 over software emulations: "The software Jupiter 8, however capable it is, is nowhere near as good as the hardware. It's in another realm." [3]
- The Korg Polysix was a point of contention between Doherty (enthusiastic) and Cook (dismissive). Doherty: "It sounds broken, but so obviously a Polysix as soon as you hear it... it's got that crackly kind of muffled top end and the filter only works to a certain level. It just has so much personality to it." The band found specific "Polysix moments" for a few EOE tracks. [3]
- Cook summarised the key EOE production philosophy shift from TBOWYTB: "On the first one, we only had those three synths — the Voyager, the Prophet 08 and a Juno 106 — and we were trying to make the most out of them by layering them and distorting them. With the second record, we wanted to make it more stripped-back, so less elements in the mix, but allowing them to breathe and allowing the character to come out a bit." [3]
- Lauren Mayberry noted the disparity between the two EOE recording suites — Cook and Doherty's side of the studio had "walls and walls of synths" while she and Simpson had only a computer and a foam booth. Mayberry: "My instrument is inside myself." [3]
- In the first two months of writing for Every Open Eye, the band progressed from no songs to approximately twenty. Doherty: "I don't remember the first two months of recording, because every day was like bang, bang, bang, bang, bang. After two months we had gone from nothing to 20-ish tunes." The band's initial anxiety about returning from two years on the road with "wasted" songwriting muscles proved unfounded; Doherty described it as "like turning a tap on." [4]
- The SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio describes Alucard Studios as "a converted three-bedroom flat on the southside of Glasgow," adding the specific room count and the descriptor "converted" to previously documented descriptions of the studio as a basement flat. [5]
- Some ideas CHVRCHES developed into EOE tracks originated as recordings made in venues during the TBOWYTB touring period; these were used as starting points alongside new material written from scratch in the studio. [5]
- EOE lyrics were written last in the composition process, after the music was complete; they were developed from words and sentences Lauren Mayberry had kept in notebooks while on tour. [5]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Eurosonic Noorderslag, 10 January 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Eurosonic Noorderslag on 10 January 2013 at Academie Minerva in Groningen, Netherlands. [1]
- The set lasted approximately 25 minutes. [1]
- "Lies" appeared early in the set. [1]
- "The Mother We Share" was the closing song of the set. [1]
- The two non-vocalist members used synthesizers, drum machines, and a laptop during the Eurosonic performance. [1]

Caption: Every Open Eye (Album Release)
Facts:
- Every Open Eye is CHVRCHES' second studio album, released in September 2015. The album title is taken from a line in the track "Clearest Blue"; Mayberry stated it was also chosen to reflect the fact that she felt constantly watched and analyzed during the band's rise. [1,2,3,4]
- In January 2015, CHVRCHES began working on new material for Every Open Eye at Alucard Studios, which they were able to upgrade thanks to the success of The Bones of What You Believe. [2]
- The production of Every Open Eye took inspiration from Quincy Jones' lean yet massive-sounding production style; Martin Doherty cited the arrangements of "Smooth Criminal" and "Billie Jean" (each approximately 8 tracks) as a revelation that drove him to strip songs that had grown to 80 tracks back to their essentials. [2,5]
- Every Open Eye debuted at number four in the U.K. [2]
- Every Open Eye reached number eight on the Billboard 200 Albums chart in the U.S. [2,6]
- Every Open Eye became a Top Ten hit in Australia, Ireland, Scotland, and New Zealand. [2]
- Every Open Eye debuted at #1 on Billboard's Top Rock Albums and Alternative Albums charts (both dated October 17, 2015), with 34,000 copies sold; on the Alternative Albums chart, it spent 7 weeks. [6,7]
- Every Open Eye was released on 2015-09-25 in Australia on the Vertigo label. It entered the Australian album chart on 2015-10-11, peaked at #3, and spent 6 weeks on the chart — corroborating the "Top Ten hit in Australia" claim. [8]
- The Australian commercial single release dates for Every Open Eye singles, per the australian-charts.com discography, were: "Leave A Trace" (Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol, Australia 2015-07-20), "Empty Threat" (Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol, Australia 2015-11-02), and "Clearest Blue" (Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol, Australia 2016-02-29). All three carried the multi-label imprint shared by the parent album. [8]
- Every Open Eye was mixed by Spike Stent, whose credits include U2, Beyoncé, and Björk; Dave Simpson served as recording engineer. [5,9]
- By the time of the EOE release (2015), CHVRCHES had established themselves as festival regulars, having performed at Glastonbury, T in the Park, SXSW, and The Great Escape; their most recent major festival appearance at time of publication was Lollapalooza (2015). [10]
- Every Open Eye was released on 2015-09-25 via Glassnote / Virgin (as described in the SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio); the Australian release was on Virgin's Vertigo imprint. [11,8]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Falls Festival 2018/2019
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were confirmed as one of five headline acts for Falls Festival 2018/2019 alongside Catfish and the Bottlemen, Vance Joy, Hilltop Hoods, and Anderson .Paak & The Free Nationals; the lineup announcement was published by triple j on 30 August 2018. [1]
- Falls Festival 2018/2019 took place over New Year's at four Australian locations: Lorne (Victoria), Marion Bay (Tasmania), Byron Bay (New South Wales), and Fremantle (Western Australia). [1]
- The Lorne (Victoria) leg of Falls Festival 2018/2019 sold out, with tickets still available for the Byron Bay, Marion Bay, and Fremantle legs at the time of the article's update. [1]
- Sydney Opera House (2019-01-07) served as the final date of CHVRCHES' Australian touring run alongside Falls Festival 2018/2019; the reviewer confirms attending a separate Falls Festival show in addition to this standalone headliner. [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Field Day 2019, The Domain, Sydney
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were confirmed on the Field Day 2019 lineup; the lineup announcement was published by triple j on 28 August 2018. [1]
- Field Day 2019 took place on Tuesday 1 January 2019 at The Domain, Sydney, and was the festival's 18th anniversary edition. [1]
- CHVRCHES' Field Day 2019 booking was framed in the announcement as their "Australian return" following their Splendour in the Grass appearance earlier in 2018. [1]
- Tickets for Field Day 2019 went on sale on Thursday 30 August 2018. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Fuji Rock Festival, July 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES headlined the White Stage at Fuji Rock Festival in July 2018; the article places this before August 3, 2018, describing the "Out Of My Head" digital release as having occurred "after Fuji Rock." [1]
- CHVRCHES briefly trended at #1 on Twitter in Japan following their White Stage headline performance at Fuji Rock 2018. [1]

Caption: "Get Out" (Song)
Facts:
- "Get Out" is a single from Love Is Dead. [1]
- "Get Out" was released as a single in the US on approximately 2018-01-31, based on an Alternative Press news report dated that day describing it as having "just dropped"; the Australian commercial single release followed on 2018-02-02. [2,3]
- The opening synth riff of "Get Out" was the first musical idea to emerge on CHVRCHES' first day in the studio with producer Greg Kurstin, as stated by the band via Alternative Press (originally sourced from Entertainment Weekly). [2]
- "Get Out" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2018-02-02 on the Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol imprint, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. [3]
- "Get Out" debuted and peaked at #17 on the Hot Rock Songs chart in February 2018. [4]
- "Get Out" peaked at #15 on the Alternative Songs airplay chart. [4]
- A Roosevelt Remix of "Get Out" was included as a Japan-exclusive bonus track on the Love Is Dead Japan Deluxe Edition (HSE-6864, Hostess Entertainment, September 2018). [5]

Caption: CHVRCHES Sign to Glassnote Records
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES signed to Glassnote Records, the same label as Mumford & Sons, in January 2013. The Under the Radar account (2015) states the signing occurred "by the end of the year" [2012], possibly reflecting late-2012 negotiations finalised in January 2013. [1,2,3]
- Lauren Mayberry stated in a 2013 interview that she valued Glassnote because Daniel Glass — the person whose name is on the label — is personally and actively involved in the record label, noting this is rare in an industry where many labels "have been sold off over and over and like smashed together." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry used her legal training to read through the CHVRCHES record contract in full and provide brief notes during the signing process. [1]

Caption: "God''s Plan" (Song)
